channel.title: RSS Aggregator Feed
channel.description: An aggregated feed of Technology-related news

item.title: Barry Diller trusts Sam Altman. But ‘trust is irrelevant’ as AGI nears, he says.
item.description: Barry Diller defended OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, while warning that AGI remains an unpredictable force needing guardrails.

item.title: Snap says its $400M deal with Perplexity ‘amicably ended’
item.description: The deal, announced last November, would have seen Perplexity's AI search engine integrated directly into Snapchat.

item.title: Is xAI a neocloud now?
item.description: xAI's real business may be more about building data centers than training AI models.

item.title: Microsoft a payé une étude pour démolir le MacBook Neo, et ça se voit
item.description: Le MacBook Neo dérange visiblement Microsoft. L'éditeur de Redmond a commandé une étude censée prouver que les PC Windows écrasent le petit Mac à 699 € : la démonstration tient surtout par ce qu'elle laisse hors champ.

item.title: 'Putting the whole Earth into a computer’: Chinese scientists use supercomputers to solve one of Yellowstone’s most enduring volcanic mysteries — and now want to create a digital twin of our planet to predict its future
item.description: Chinese researchers used supercomputers to model Yellowstone, revealing that tectonic forces shaped magma pathways and sparking ambitions for full planetary simulation systems.

item.title: Canadian officials claim OpenAI violated federal and provincial privacy laws
item.description: Regulators took issue with the amount of personal data the company collected and its approach to consent.

item.title: A Kid With a Fake Mustache Tricked an Online Age-Verification Tool
item.description: To stop children from bypassing its age checks, Meta is revamping its age-verification tools with an AI system that analyzes images and videos for “visual cues,” such as height and bone structure.

item.title: Google shuts down Project Mariner
item.description: Google has pulled the plug on Project Mariner, an experimental feature designed to perform tasks for you across the web, as reported earlier by Wired's Maxwell Zeff. The Project Mariner landing page now contains a message that says: "Thank you for using Project Mariner. It was shut down on May 4th, 2026 and its technology […]

item.title: Snap's $400 million deal with Perplexity is dead
item.description: The planned AI search feature for Snapchat never fully rolled out.

item.title: ‘AI capabilities that did not exist at the time’: Delayed Siri features have cost Apple a massive $250 million, and iPhone users could get up to $95 per device
item.description: Apple is paying $250 million due to delayed Siri features, with affected users eligible for up to $95 per device.

item.title: Insurance startup Corgi hits $1.3B valuation 4 months after its Series A
item.description: Corgi announced on Wednesday a $160 million Series B, led by TCV, valuing the startup at $1.3 billion.

item.title: AMD's CEO predicts 'higher memory and component costs' later this year — so brace yourself for Radeon GPU price hikes
item.description: Following Lisa Su's revelation, a disgruntled Redditor predicted: 'The RAM economy will permanently change and data centers will be a nuisance for the rest of recorded time'.

item.title: Salesforce says it will hire 1,000 ‘AI-native’ new grads
item.description: The company is fast-tracking young talent into AI roles who are familiar with the technology amid a brutal entry-level job market. Last week, Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff announced in a post on X that the company would hire 1,000 new grads and interns “to ride the AI exponential.” Today, the company released a statement committing to that plan.

item.title: NYT Connections decided not to use words, and players are not OK
item.description: The New York Times' word game Connections was decidedly lacking in words on Wednesday, and the internet had thoughts.

item.title: AI can make work more meaningful
item.description: Companies should capitalize on the efficiencies AI offers to change how people experience their work. As a CEO operating within the global supply chain—where every purchase is tied to efforts to end forced and child labor—I think often about what work is for: not just making it faster, but making it matter.

item.title: Apple reaches $250 million settlement over exaggerating Apple Intelligences capabilities
item.description: Apple has reached a $250 million settlement in a lawsuit that accused the company of exaggerating Apple Intelligence's intelligence.

item.title: How David Sacks crashed and burned in the White House
item.description: Hello and welcome to Regulator, a newsletter exclusively for Verge subscribers about tech, politics, and Washington intrigue. (It's basically House of Cards, but for nerds.) Not a subscriber yet? You really should become one, and to save you a Google search, here is the direct link to do so! And do you think I should […]

item.title: Microsoft may abandon one of its climate goals. Blame data centers.
item.description: The insatiable costs of data centers is making some tech companies pull back their clean energy goals.

item.title: 'Change in technology that will reshape medicine': Leading medical experts say new AI reasoning models are so good they outperform even the best specialist doctors when it comes to diagnosing some of the world's most mysterious cases
item.description: AI diagnostic models show strong performance on complex cases, though limitations in handling uncertainty raise concerns about real-world clinical use.
